title: Why Indonesian Businesses Should Go Global: The Diaspora Opportunity in 2026
metaDescription: Discover why 2026 is the best time for Indonesian businesses to expand globally. 6 million Indonesians overseas represent a massive untapped market with low digital competition.
content:
The 6 Million Opportunity Most Indonesian Businesses Are Missing

There are over 6 million Indonesians living abroad — in Malaysia, Saudi Arabia, Japan, Australia, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, and dozens of other countries. These are your potential customers. They buy Indonesian food. They send money home through Indonesian remittance services. They wear batik. They miss the flavours of home.

And yet, the vast majority of Indonesian businesses have never even tried to reach them.

This isn't just a missed opportunity — it's arguably the biggest underexploited market available to Indonesian entrepreneurs today.

Why the Timing Is Perfect in 2026

Three trends are converging right now that make overseas expansion more accessible than ever before:

  • Digital marketing costs overseas are low. In markets like Qatar, Brunei, and even Japan, there are almost no Indonesian businesses competing for the same Google keywords. You can rank Page 1 in months, not years.
  • The Indonesian diaspora is growing and spending more. Remittances from Indonesians abroad reached $10.6 billion in 2025. This is money being circulated within the community — and much of it flows through businesses that understand Indonesian culture.
  • WhatsApp and social media eliminate the geographic barrier. You don't need a physical presence overseas. A well-run Instagram, a WhatsApp number, and a fast website are enough to serve customers in Kuala Lumpur, Riyadh, or Amsterdam.

Which Countries Have the Largest Indonesian Diaspora?

Understanding where your potential customers are is the first step:

  • Malaysia — over 1 million Indonesians, the largest diaspora community
  • Saudi Arabia — 2 million+, predominantly working in domestic, hospitality, and construction sectors
  • UAE — 200,000+ in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, and Sharjah
  • Qatar — 250,000+ in Doha and surrounding cities
  • Kuwait — 200,000+, primarily domestic workers and service sector
  • Japan — 150,000+, growing student and professional community
  • Taiwan — 300,000+, the largest Indonesian community outside ASEAN
  • Australia — 100,000+, highly educated, high spending power
  • Netherlands — 450,000+ with Dutch-Indonesian heritage, deep cultural ties
  • United Kingdom — 50,000+, strong professional community in London

What Industries Have the Highest Demand Overseas?

Not every business type will succeed equally in every market. Based on real results from hundreds of overseas Indonesian businesses, these sectors consistently show the strongest demand:

  • Halal food and catering — especially in Gulf countries, Japan, and Europe where Indonesian cuisine is rare and highly sought-after
  • Remittance and financial services — communities abroad constantly need to send money home
  • Muslim fashion and modest wear — growing demand across all diaspora markets
  • Travel, Umroh, and Hajj services — extremely high demand in the Gulf region
  • Education and language services — Bahasa Indonesia lessons, Indonesian tutoring
  • Online retail — Indonesian products, snacks, spices, and consumer goods

The Keyword Competition Advantage

Here is something most Indonesian business owners don't know: ranking on Google in overseas Indonesian-niche markets is dramatically easier than ranking in Indonesia.

Searching for “Indonesian restaurant Tokyo” on Google Japan? There might be 3 websites competing. Searching for “jasa catering Indonesia Doha Qatar”? Almost nobody is doing SEO for that term.

Compare this to Indonesia, where ranking for “jasa catering Jakarta” means competing against hundreds of businesses who have been doing SEO for years. The effort required to reach the first page is dramatically lower overseas.

How to Start: A Practical Framework

Going global doesn't have to be complicated. Here's a simplified framework:

  • Step 1 — Pick your target country. Choose one market first. Start with Malaysia (language proximity) or Saudi Arabia (largest diaspora) depending on your industry.
  • Step 2 — Build your digital presence for that market. This means a localised website page, Google My Business listing for the target city, and social media content in the right language.
  • Step 3 — Run targeted paid ads. Facebook and Instagram allow precise geographic and interest-based targeting. A modest budget of $500-1,000/month can generate significant reach in diaspora communities.
  • Step 4 — Build community trust. WhatsApp groups, community events, and consistent content that speaks to diaspora pain points builds the trust needed for purchase decisions.
